title: Procrastination Test
description: Use six quick prompts to understand whether procrastination is driven by avoidance, planning gaps, or energy drain.
introTitle: How the last week actually felt
intro: Rate each statement from 1 to 5. Go with your first honest answer.

low.title: Low procrastination risk
low.description: Your rhythm looks stable. Delays seem more situational than structural.

medium.title: Some procrastination patterns
medium.description: The issue is still manageable, but it is starting to weaken consistency.

high.title: Procrastination is affecting delivery
high.description: Without a workflow change, tasks will keep slipping later.

critical.title: Procrastination load is high
critical.description: Focus first on task shrinkage, recovery, and a gentler execution rhythm.

low: Keep a fixed starting ritual, even if it is only a three-minute draft.
Reserve high-energy time for tasks that are hardest to begin.
End each day by defining tomorrow's first visible output.
medium: Break work into 15-minute chunks and commit only to the first chunk.
Use a timer to reduce switching and early browsing drift.
Track when procrastination happens so the trigger becomes visible.
high: Replace vague goals with one concrete deliverable per session.
Create an earlier checkpoint so urgency does not arrive all at once.
Limit active work to one main task and one backup task.
critical: Rebuild basic recovery first. Low sleep and overload can look like procrastination.
Set just one must-do item per day before adding extras.
Add external accountability through check-ins, sharing progress, or review rituals.

questions.label: When an important task is waiting, I start with side chores instead.
questions.hint: Formatting, inbox cleanup, desk tidying, and similar warmups.

questions.label: Once work feels difficult, I get pulled into my phone or browser quickly.
questions.hint: Especially in the first few minutes.

questions.label: I delay because I cannot see a clear first step.
questions.hint: The task feels too large to enter.

questions.label: My focus only spikes once the deadline becomes urgent.
questions.hint: Before that, I keep pushing it back.

questions.label: Even when I know what matters, I feel too drained to start.
questions.hint: This is more fatigue than lack of importance.

questions.label: Too many open obligations make me freeze instead of act.
